pair_convention.spot_explanation: AUD/USD at 0.7015 means 1 AUD buys 0.7015 USD. This is a XXX/USD convention (Pattern 1): a rising AUD/USD rate means the Australian Dollar is strengthening relative to the US Dollar (each AUD purchases more USD), while a falling rate means the AUD is weakening and purchasing fewer USD. A move from 0.7015 to 0.7100 would represent AUD appreciation; a move from 0.7015 to 0.6900 would represent AUD depreciation.

executive_summary: AUD/USD trades at 0.7015 (latest close 2026-06-19), having fallen -2.07% over 1M and -1.58% over 3M while sitting roughly flat over 6M (+0.03%) and up +4.89% over 1Y — a pair that surged to a multi-year high near 0.7280 in early May before rolling over into a corrective consolidation. The dominant bilateral narrative is a rare and genuinely conflicting setup: both currencies' standalone dossiers turned tactically Bullish on their own currency, which mutes rather than amplifies the pair signal. The AUD leg rests on still-high absolute carry (RBA 4.35%) plus a risk-on de-escalation tailwind, while the USD leg rests on a freshly hawkish Warsh-led Fed that flipped the 2026 dot plot from cuts to a likely hike, with Fed funds futures now pricing around 77% probability of a rate hike by December 2026, up sharply from 24% a month ago, despite the recent collapse in oil prices. The defining bilateral differential is the collapse of AUD's front-end carry edge: the RBA-Fed gap has compressed to roughly +56-72bp and is now narrowing from BOTH sides — the RBA is losing its imported-inflation rationale as oil collapses while the Fed is pricing hikes. The same US-Iran de-escalation that supports high-beta AUD via risk appetite simultaneously empowers the hawkish-Fed dollar, leaving the two transmission channels offsetting. Our headline stance is mildly Bearish AUD/USD tactically — the hawkish-Fed repricing and negative AUD positioning momentum outweigh the still-positive but eroding carry — shading to Neutral strategically as rich AUD valuation and the disappearing rate differential offset structural USD twin-deficit drag. Conviction is low: this is a tug-of-war between two firm currencies, not a clean directional trend.

market_context.valuation_assessment: On the bilateral REER differential, AUD is the richer of the two: the AUD real broad REER printed 115.86 in April 2026 (95th percentile, firmest in the G10 ex-SGD) versus the USD REER at 107.06 (78th percentile). The PPP signal is two-sided — OECD PPP implies AUD/USD fair value near 0.73 (spot ~3% cheap to USD-PPP), yet on the REER cross the USD screens as roughly 8% cheaper than AUD. The net read: both currencies are rich on a trade-weighted basis, but AUD is richer, which structurally caps further AUD/USD upside and means a great deal of carry-positive news is already embedded in spot near 0.70. Bank consensus broadly agrees the pair is range-bound, with major banks forecasting AUD/USD in the 0.69 to 0.73 range, and Westpac, NAB, and CBA projecting the AUD to trade in the 0.69 to 0.72 range in 2026, with CBA allowing for upside to 0.73.

market_context.recent_regime: The pair's recent behaviour is dominated by the collision of two simultaneous mid-June shocks that, for AUD/USD specifically, largely cancel: the US-Iran de-escalation (risk-on, AUD-supportive) and the hawkish Warsh Fed (USD-supportive). The net result has been a grind lower from the May high toward 0.70 as the dollar leg won the near-term tug-of-war. The RBA kept its cash rate on hold at 4.35% on 16 June after three consecutive hikes but warned the hiking cycle may be over, providing some AUD support, though the hawkish Fed is likely to dominate near-term. The pair consolidates near 0.70-0.71 as still-high AUD carry defends dips while the dollar's revived rate narrative caps rallies.

bilateral_carry_analysis.carry_assessment: The carry story is the single most important bilateral differential — and it is fading from both ends. The RBA-Fed policy gap is +72bp (RBA 4.35% vs Fed midpoint 3.625%), while the cleaner market-rate measure, the AU-US 2Y spread, is ~+56bp (AU 4.63% vs US 4.07% pre-FOMC; post-FOMC US 2Y near 4.20% compresses this toward ~+43bp). The AU-US 10Y spread is a thin +9 to +32bp depending on snapshot. This is a modest carry advantage being squeezed simultaneously from both sides: the RBA is losing its imported-inflation hawkishness as oil collapses (market pricing end-of-cycle), while the Fed is now pricing hikes. The carry-to-vol ratio of ~0.072 is thin and unattractive versus AUD's JPY/CHF carry crosses (43-56). AUD/USD is the weakest carry expression of AUD's rate advantage; the decisive bilateral point is that the hawkish Fed repricing directly attacks the AUD/USD carry from the quote side, something neither the AUD-vs-JPY nor AUD-vs-CHF carry faces. If the Fed is forced to hike while the RBA pauses, the differential narrows and AUD faces significant headwinds. The forward discount of ~-0.72%/yr (CIP-consistent) means holders earn modest positive carry but face roll-down, and the thin ~42-pip breakeven offers almost no cushion against the tactical headwinds.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: This is where the two Bullish standalone views collide most directly, and the net for the pair is mildly Bearish. The AUD-US rate differential (~+56bp 2Y, ~+72bp policy) still favours AUD in absolute terms, but the trajectory is the story: the differential is NARROWING from both sides. On the AUD side, the RBA's 16 June hawkish hold now looks backward-looking as the oil collapse removes the imported-inflation driver, and the market is beginning to price the end of the hiking cycle. On the USD side, the Fed flipped decisively hawkish: the FOMC June 2026 dot plot sees the end-of-year target at 3.8% vs 3.4% in March, implying roughly one hike. The transmission to AUD/USD is direct and adverse: the quote-side rate repricing lifts US yields, compresses AUD's carry edge, and reasserts the dollar. With the front-end gap thin and shrinking, the rate factor that anchors the AUD bull case is precisely the factor most undermined bilaterally — the Fed is closing the gap that the RBA can no longer widen.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 1-6 months the rate factor neutralises for the pair. The AUD dossier sees the RBA's hawkish premise eroding (carry fade realized), while the USD dossier sees the Fed's hawkish edge fading as synchronized global tightening compresses the dollar's relative advantage and cheaper oil slows US inflation. Both central banks are therefore expected to be at or past their peaks within the horizon, leaving the absolute differential roughly stable-to-narrowing but with no clear directional widening for either side. The key swing is sequencing: if the Fed actually hikes in July/September while the RBA holds, the differential collapses toward zero (Bearish AUD/USD); if the post-ceasefire disinflation hits the US first and the Fed reverts to the 2027-cut narrative while the RBA holds 4.35%, the gap re-widens (Bullish AUD/USD). With both outcomes live, the strategic rate factor is a balanced Neutral. The structural AU-DE and term-premium cushions that support AUD on crosses do not help AUD/USD, where the quote currency itself carries a top-tier rate.

carry_analysis.summary: The AUD/USD carry is the weakest expression of AUD's rate advantage. The +72bp policy gap and ~+56bp 2Y spread are being compressed from both sides — the RBA losing its oil-inflation rationale while the Fed prices hikes. Carry-to-vol of ~0.072 is far inferior to AUD/JPY (~43) and AUD/CHF (~56). The ~42-pip carry breakeven offers almost no cushion, and the hawkish-Fed repricing directly attacks AUD/USD carry from the quote side — something AUD crosses against low-yielders do not face.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: Both economies are running hot, energy-driven headline inflation, which is the unusual symmetry that neutralises this factor for the pair. AU headline CPI is ~4.05-4.6% YoY with trimmed mean 3.3%; US headline CPI hit 4.2% YoY with core PCE 3.3%. Both prints were fuel-driven and both are now set to roll over post-ceasefire — but the bilateral asymmetry is that the US faces a hawkish Fed acting on its inflation while the RBA is pivoting to acknowledge the oil-driven disinflation. Growth differentials are negligible: AU GDP +2.52% YoY versus US +2.57% YoY, essentially identical, though US exceptionalism (ISM surveys in expansion, GDPNow ~3%) gives the dollar a marginal relative-growth edge. The pivotal bilateral event is the asymmetric data sensitivity: the 29 July Australian Q2 CPI is skewed to a soft, disinflationary surprise (Bearish AUD), while the US 25 June core PCE and 14 July CPI are watched for whether the Fed-hike rationale holds (sticky = Bullish USD = Bearish AUD/USD). Both data vectors point the same direction for the pair — mildly down — yet the offsetting hot-inflation symmetry keeps the factor Neutral rather than Bearish.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically the inflation-growth picture remains symmetric and Neutral for the pair. Both economies are expected to disinflate as the oil shock resolves, and both central banks move toward the end of their tightening. The bilateral question is which disinflates faster and which central bank reacts first — and on current evidence that is genuinely two-sided. US exceptionalism (resilient labour market, expansionary ISMs) is the more durable relative-growth support for the dollar, marginally Bearish for AUD/USD, but Australia's solid +2.52% growth and tight labour market keep its underlying inflation sticky enough that the RBA need not rush to cut. Neither economy offers a decisive growth or inflation divergence to drive the pair over the horizon.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: AUD/USD is a moderate-to-high risk-beta pair (high-beta AUD vs safe-haven USD), so the de-escalation regime is net Bullish for the pair via the risk channel — the one factor that unambiguously favours AUD here. The US-Iran ceasefire collapsed the geopolitical premium, eased VIX to ~16.2 from ~22, and lifted equities, which simultaneously bids high-beta AUD AND drains the USD safe-haven premium — a double tailwind for AUD/USD. The dossiers confirm the bilateral mechanism: the AUD dossier flips Risk Appetite to Bullish on the de-escalation, while the USD dossier flips its Risk factor to Bearish (haven unwind). The two reinforce for the pair (AUD up). The complication is that this risk-on impulse is partially offset within the pair by the hawkish Fed reasserting the right side of the dollar smile (US exceptionalism), but on the pure risk-sentiment axis the signal is clearly AUD/USD-positive. The persistent -0.71 AUD/USD risk reversal is a lagging downside hedge that should flatten if calm persists.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically the risk factor is Neutral for the pair, lower-weighted (0.05). The oil-shock risk-off tail that would have hammered high-beta AUD has been removed, which is structurally AUD/USD-supportive, but two offsetting tails remain: the fragile 60-day ceasefire (a breakdown snaps the USD haven bid back on, Bearish AUD/USD) and the dated 10 November US-China tariff cliff (which would hit AUD as the most liquid China proxy AND restore the USD haven bid — doubly Bearish AUD/USD). These China/geopolitical tails are asymmetrically negative for the pair because they hit the base currency's growth proxy and lift the quote currency's haven status simultaneously. The risk-on base case modestly favours AUD/USD, but the convex left-tail keeps the strategic read Neutral.

On the AUD leg, CFTC leveraged funds slashed net longs by 15,209 contracts to +42,292 (~35th percentile) in the week to 9 June while asset managers flipped to net short -12,083 — clear negative momentum into the stalling rally. On the USD leg, specs rebuilt dollar longs into the hawkish Fed (~60th-65th percentile). The net signal: AUD positioning momentum is deteriorating while USD positioning is building, both pointing the pair mildly lower, but neither is at an extreme, so this is a momentum read rather than a contrarian setup.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The terms-of-trade and bilateral trade picture nets to Neutral tactically. Australia's key export, iron ore 62% Fe (~$98/t), is range-bound and oil-insensitive, so the dominant AUD ToT anchor is steady. The energy-price collapse is roughly neutral for AUD (lower LNG/coal export values offset by a cheaper refined-fuel import bill) but is a mild negative for the USD as a net energy exporter (lower energy-export receipts) — a marginal AUD/USD positive that is too small to move spot over weeks. On bilateral trade, the IMF DOTS data show Australia runs a structural goods deficit with the US: iron ore and energy exports support national income, but AUD exports to the US were only $15.7bn vs $35.6bn imports from the US (2024), a net goods balance of -$19.9bn. This bilateral deficit is a slow structural drag on AUD/USD but is not a tactical driver. Net commodity exposure: oil↓ is mildly AUD-negative (export values) but more USD-negative (energy exporter), netting marginally AUD/USD-positive; iron ore steady is neutral.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: At the heaviest strategic weight (0.16), the external picture is a rare Neutral for the pair because BOTH currencies carry similar structural external drags. Australia runs a -3.68% current account; the US runs a ~-3.6% current account plus a ~5.8% fiscal deficit (the twin deficit). The AUD bear case (capped, oil-insensitive iron ore; soft China steel; rich REER) is genuinely offset by the USD bear case (twin-deficit gravity; reserve diversification; rich REER at 107). The bilateral net commodity rule (oil↓) is marginally AUD/USD-positive since the US is a net energy exporter while Australia's energy swing is internally hedged by its import bill. China is the swing variable for the AUD leg (~30-35% of exports) and is stabilising not re-accelerating. With both external positions structurally weak and roughly comparable, neither side wins decisively — a balanced Neutral, which is itself notable given how heavily this factor weighs at the strategic horizon.

net_commodity_exposure.base_commodity_profile: AUD is commodity-positive: iron ore (~35% of exports, ~$98/t, range-bound), coal and LNG (energy-export revenues, falling with oil), lithium and copper (firm). Australia is a net energy exporter but LNG/coal are partially offset by refined-fuel import savings when oil falls.
net_commodity_exposure.quote_commodity_profile: USD is a net energy exporter (oil, LNG): lower oil prices reduce US energy-export receipts, a mild USD-negative. The US is not significantly exposed to iron ore or base metals as an export earner.
net_commodity_exposure.net_effect_calculation: Step 1 — Iron ore (~$98/t, stable): AUD-positive, USD-neutral → net AUD/USD-positive but small (range-bound, no directional impulse). Step 2 — Oil collapse: AUD-negative (lower LNG/coal export values) but offset by cheaper import bill → approximately neutral for AUD. USD-negative (lower energy export receipts, net energy exporter) → mild USD-negative. Net oil-channel: marginally AUD/USD-positive because the US energy-exporter loss is larger than Australia's oil-adjusted net. Step 3 — Coal/lithium firm: mildly AUD-positive, USD-neutral → small net AUD/USD-positive. Overall net: marginally AUD/USD-positive from the oil-↓ channel, but the magnitude is small (~negligible over 2-4 weeks). Neither currency is predominantly oil-driven in the way a NOK or CAD would be.
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_up_10pct: Oil +10%: AUD-positive (higher LNG/coal export revenues), USD-positive (higher energy receipts) — roughly offsetting, net effect on AUD/USD near zero or marginally AUD/USD-negative if USD energy benefit exceeds AUD's
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_down_10pct: Oil -10%: AUD-negative (lower LNG/coal), USD-negative (lower energy exports), net marginal AUD/USD-positive as US net-energy-exporter loss slightly exceeds AUD's partially-hedged impact
net_commodity_exposure.dominant_commodity_driver: Iron ore (62% Fe, ~$98/t) is the dominant AUD-specific driver for AUD/USD because it is oil-insensitive, constitutes ~35% of Australian export revenue, and is not a significant US export — making iron ore price the cleanest AUD ToT signal against the USD. China demand for iron ore is the key swing variable.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: Both central banks delivered hawkish guidance in the same week, but the bilateral net is mildly Bearish AUD/USD because the Fed's hawkish shift was the larger SURPRISE. The RBA's 16 June hawkish hold was largely expected and its premise (oil inflation) is already eroding; the Fed's pivot under new Chair Warsh was a genuine regime change — Kevin Warsh took office as chairman of the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve in May 2026, and his anti-inflation debut plus the raised dot plot reset market expectations from cuts to a likely hike. The bilateral credibility differential temporarily favours the USD: the market is taking Warsh's hawkish framing at face value, reinforcing the dollar against an RBA whose hawkish guidance the market increasingly expects to be walked back at the 11 August SoMP. Both guidance vectors point the pair lower near-term.
key_bilateral_metrics: Fed dot plot raised to 3.8% (surprise) vs RBA expected hawkish hold (priced)
Warsh anti-inflation debut = fresh USD credibility positive
RBA hawkish premise eroding (oil collapse); 11 Aug SoMP likely softer
Both CBs hawkish, but Fed shift is the larger surprise → Bearish AUD/USD
Next checkpoints: RBA June minutes; FOMC 29 July; RBA SoMP 11 Aug
tactical.what_is_priced: The market has priced both hawkish messages but is leaning into the Fed's as the more durable. The bilateral edge: the Fed's surprise hawkishness outweighs the RBA's expected (and eroding) hawkishness, mildly Bearish for the pair.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically the guidance factor is Neutral for the pair, balancing two opposing credibility risks. The RBA retains high relative credibility (transparent, data-dependent, delivered three 2026 hikes) but its hawkish bias is set to soften as oil-driven disinflation lands. The Fed's strategic credibility is the wild card: the Warsh-led, Trump-appointed Fed carries an asymmetric independence tail — if the new Fed is later seen to ease for political rather than data reasons, the dollar's credibility premium erodes sharply (Bullish AUD/USD). So the bilateral strategic picture pits a credibly-softening RBA (mildly Bearish AUD/USD) against a USD independence tail (Bullish AUD/USD if it fires). These offset to Neutral, with the fat tail being a politicised Fed cut that would lift AUD/USD.
key_bilateral_metrics: RBA high relative credibility but softening bias (oil)
Fed independence tail: politicised easing = Bullish AUD/USD (asymmetric)
Warsh hawkish debut bought near-term credibility insurance
RBA still more hawkish than pre-pivot baseline; relative edge fading
Sept/Dec SEPs and 11 Aug/3 Nov RBA SoMPs = key checkpoints
strategic.what_is_priced: The market prices both as credibly hawkish for now. The bilateral edge: the asymmetric, underpriced USD independence tail (politicised cut) is the one credibility scenario that would lift AUD/USD materially — keeping this Neutral with a latent bullish tail.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical is Bearish (58) because the Fed's hawkish surprise outweighs the RBA's expected, eroding hawkishness; strategic is Neutral (54) because a credibly-softening RBA offsets the asymmetric USD independence tail (politicised Fed cut) that would lift the pair.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The tactical event calendar leans mildly Bearish AUD/USD because the binary catalysts cluster against the pair. The dominant prior tail (Hormuz escalation) is resolved. What now dominates is a dense July US/AU data cluster where the asymmetry favours USD: the AU Q2 CPI (29 July) is skewed to a soft, disinflationary surprise (Bearish AUD), while the US 25 June core PCE, 2 July NFP, 14 July CPI and 29 July FOMC are watched for whether the Fed-hike rationale holds (sticky = Bullish USD). Both data vectors point the pair down. The 31 July BoJ meeting is a tail risk that could trigger a broad risk-off (Bearish high-beta AUD/USD). The pace of Iranian oil returning is a secondary disinflation driver. The event clustering is unusually adverse: the AU and US prints fall in the same fortnight and both skew Bearish for AUD/USD.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The strategic event slate contains the single most pair-specific dated risk: the 10 November US-China tariff cliff. This is doubly Bearish for AUD/USD because it hits the base currency's China-growth proxy AND restores the quote currency's safe-haven bid simultaneously — a uniquely adverse bilateral event for this pair that matters far more for AUD/USD than for, say, EUR/GBP. Layered alongside are the mid-August Iran nuclear deadline (a breakdown is Bearish AUD/USD via the haven channel), the 1 October US funding cliff (mildly USD-negative, Bullish AUD/USD), and the 3 November US midterms (USD-ambiguous). The tariff cliff is the dominant dated catalyst and it is asymmetrically Bearish for the pair, tilting the strategic event factor Bearish.

Mid-Aug Iran nuclear deadline: breakdown Bearish AUD/USD (haven)

AUD = most liquid China proxy (acute tariff exposure)

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons are Bearish; tactical (56) on the asymmetric late-July AU-CPI-soft / US-data-sticky cluster, strategic (56) on the doubly-Bearish 10 November tariff cliff that hits AUD's China proxy and lifts the USD haven simultaneously.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The fiscal differential is a clear and durable AUD/USD-positive: Australia holds a AAA trifecta (S&P AAA, Moody's Aaa, Fitch AAA) with gross debt just 34.0% of GDP and a deficit near 1%, while the US lost its last AAA (Moody's Aa1, May 2025) and runs a ~5.8%-of-GDP deficit with debt held by the public ~101% of GDP. This is one of the widest fiscal-quality gaps in the G10 and structurally favours AUD over USD. Tactically there is no dated fiscal catalyst in the window, so the impact is marginal, but in a still-uncertain regime the AAA-vs-Aa1 differential is a quiet AUD/USD support. The low 0.04 tactical weight reflects the absence of a near-term trigger.
key_bilateral_metrics: AU: AAA trifecta, debt 34.0% GDP, deficit ~1%
US: Moody's Aa1, debt ~101% GDP, deficit ~5.8%
Widest-quality fiscal gap structurally favours AUD/USD
No dated fiscal catalyst in tactical window
AU sovereign CDS structurally tight (low-teens-20bp)
tactical.what_is_priced: Both fiscal positions are well-known and largely priced. The bilateral edge: the AAA-vs-Aa1 gap is a quiet, reliable AUD/USD-positive that compounds in any risk-aware regime.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically the fiscal differential is a durable AUD/USD support at the 0.07 weight. Australia's AAA headroom (debt 34% GDP) versus the US twin-deficit trajectory (debt toward 120% by 2036, net interest doubling, lost AAA) is a structural differentiator that grows in relevance as global fiscal-sustainability concerns build. The US faces concrete near-term fiscal noise (the 1 October FY2027 funding cliff) that Australia lacks. As fiscal gravity becomes a larger market theme, Australia's balance-sheet strength is an underappreciated strategic anchor for AUD versus the fiscally-stretched USD.

US lost last AAA (Moody's Aa1); AU retains trifecta
US FY2027 funding cliff (1 Oct) — AU has no equivalent
AU AAA underpins low-risk ACGB funding of its CA deficit
strategic.what_is_priced: The market takes both fiscal positions for granted. The bilateral edge: as fiscal sustainability rises as a theme, Australia's AAA headroom is an underpriced structural AUD/USD anchor.

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons are Bullish; strategic confidence (60) exceeds tactical (58) because the fiscal-quality gap is a slow-moving structural attribute whose AUD/USD-positive value compounds over the 1-6 month horizon, while its tactical impact is marginal absent a dated catalyst.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The pair's own chart turned corrective-bearish after the May top. Spot 0.7015 sits below the 50d (0.7138) and 100d (0.7089) and is now testing the 200d (0.6966) — the MA configuration has rolled from bullish-stacked to flattening/tangled, the structural uptrend losing thrust. From web technicals, the descending trendline near 0.7120 is the pivotal resistance, and repeated failures near the 100-day SMA favor bearish traders, with rallies more likely to be sold into while spot remains capped beneath overhead levels. Momentum indicators are soft: the RSI is near 42, and a slightly negative MACD reading hints at waning upside momentum. The risk reversal (-0.71) shows the options market still hedging AUD/USD downside. With cheap IV (~35th percentile) and a clear lower-high structure, the structural read is mildly Bearish for the pair near-term.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically the structure is Neutral and provides context, not direction. The year-long uptrend (+4.89% 1Y) has matured into a high-level range, with the 200d (0.6966) marking the structural trend line systematic funds watch. A sustained break below 0.6945 would flip CTAs short and confirm a strategic top (Bearish); a reclaim above 0.7280 would signal a fresh up-leg (Bullish). Vol term structure in contango with cheap longer-dated IV implies the market expects only gradual range broadening. The bigger-picture technical read is corrective: a medium-term top could be forming at 0.7277 after failing to sustain above the 61.8% retracement of the 0.8006 (2021 high) to 0.5913 (2024 low) at 0.7206. Structure follows fundamentals over this horizon, so it neither confirms nor denies the view.

expression_assessment.vol_landscape_summary: AUD/USD 1M IV ~7.5% at the ~35th percentile of its long-run ~9-11% range, with 3M IV ~7.81% and realised vol (6.8%) running just below implied — a modest ~0.7pt variance risk premium. The put skew (-0.71 at 1M, steepening to -1.10 at 1Y) reflects both AUD's high-beta downside and the hawkish-Fed USD-strength tail. Vol term structure is in contango, signalling no acute near-term event premium and leaving cheap optionality available.
expression_assessment.directional_expression: Because the directional view is only mildly Bearish and conviction is low (2/5), the cleanest expression is NOT outright AUD/USD spot but rather relative-value crosses where AUD's carry advantage is cleaner (AUD/JPY, short EUR/AUD) — the AUD/USD carry-to-vol of ~0.07 is far inferior to AUD/JPY's ~43. For those expressing the view directly in AUD/USD, selling rallies toward 0.7120 resistance with cheap puts harvests the skew and hedges the tariff/Fed tails. Realised running just below implied means there is no strong variance-basis case to prefer spot over options.
expression_assessment.event_timing_consideration: The contango structure means structures can be set at the 1M-2M tenor to capture the dense late-July AU-CPI/US-data cluster (25 June US core PCE, 2 July NFP, 14 July US CPI, 29 July AU Q2 CPI and FOMC double-header). This is the primary event window and cheap implied vol makes it attractive to be long gamma into these dates rather than short delta via spot.
expression_assessment.liquidity_note: AUD/USD is a top-5 global FX pair by volume with deep, liquid spot and options markets. Execution costs are minimal and the options market is well-developed with active market-making. The pair's moderate size and deep liquidity mean large positions can be executed without material slippage, though quarter-end and year-end windows may see reduced liquidity in the crosses.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: For a USD pair, this factor acts directly through the quote leg, and conditions are benign-Neutral. SOFR is stable at ~3.63-3.69% around IORB (3.65%) with no money-market dislocation, the Fed ended QT in December 2025, and reserves are ample. There is no USD scarcity squeezing the offshore borrowers (Australian banks are structural offshore USD funders) that would push AUD/USD lower via the funding channel. The broad USD index is firm but not spiking — a mid-dollar-smile regime rather than a crisis squeeze, which is the backdrop in which high-beta AUD historically performs acceptably. The end of QT marginally eases dollar scarcity (faintly AUD/USD-positive), but absent any funding stress the factor is a quiet Neutral. Its 0.11 weight makes the absence of stress modestly reassuring for AUD/USD.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, USD liquidity leans mildly AUD/USD-positive at the 0.12 weight via the dollar-smile logic. With QT over and the Fed maintaining a large, slowly-growing balance sheet, global dollar supply is no longer being actively drained — removing a structural dollar support and tilting toward the easy-funding bottom of the smile (AUD/USD-supportive). A firm-but-not-spiking USD sitting mid-smile is precisely the regime in which AUD's carry can express. The main risk is a year-end/quarter-end issuance- or tariff-driven funding squeeze that would widen the basis and pressure AUD/USD via the offshore-borrowing channel (the convex left-tail). Net mildly Bullish AUD/USD on the non-tightening Fed, contingent on no funding-stress event.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical is Neutral (55) because USD funding shows no current stress; strategic is Bullish (52) because the post-QT, mid-dollar-smile regime with abundant liquidity is the backdrop in which AUD's carry can express in AUD/USD, contingent on no funding squeeze.

tactical.score_interpretation: Net score -0.227 sits at the boundary of the mildly-Bearish band, led by the heavyweight Policy & Rates factor (-0.154) as the hawkish Fed compresses AUD's carry edge, reinforced by Flows (-0.058), Guidance (-0.041), Structure (-0.028) and Event Risk (-0.028). The only meaningful offsets are Risk Appetite (+0.058, the de-escalation double-tailwind) and Fiscal (+0.023). This maps to conviction 2 — a low-conviction directional lean, not a high-conviction call, reflecting that two firm currencies are pulling the pair in opposite directions.
tactical.rationale: At tactical weights the score is led by Policy & Rates (-0.154), the decisive bilateral factor: AUD's still-positive but thin carry edge (~+56bp) is being compressed from the quote side by the hawkish Warsh Fed, which priced the 2026 dot plot to 3.8% and lifted hike odds to ~77%. This is the rare case where both standalone dossiers are Bullish on their own currency, which mutes rather than amplifies the pair — and where they conflict, the heavier-weight, larger-surprise factor (the Fed pivot) wins. The bullish AUD factors do not transmit cleanly to AUD/USD: AUD's exceptional carry-to-vol is a JPY/CHF-cross phenomenon, worth only ~0.07 against the high-yielding USD. The base and quote views interact destructively for the pair: the same US-Iran de-escalation that turns AUD's risk factor Bullish also empowers the hawkish-Fed dollar, so Risk Appetite (+0.058) is the one factor cleanly favouring AUD/USD while Flows, Guidance, Structure and Event Risk all lean mildly Bearish. What is priced versus what we expect: the market has moved ~two-thirds toward a Fed hike and is fading AUD via fresh shorts, so the easy down-move is partly done; our edge is that the late-July AU-CPI-soft / US-data-sticky catalyst cluster skews asymmetrically Bearish for the pair, and the thin ~42-pip carry breakeven offers almost no cushion. The path of least resistance is a grind toward 0.69-0.70 with rallies toward 0.7120 sold. Key dated catalysts: 25 June US core PCE, 2 July US NFP, 14 July US CPI, 29 July AU Q2 CPI and the same-day FOMC. Conviction drivers: the alignment of rates, flows, guidance and structure pointing the pair down; the conviction cap (holding at 2): the genuinely Bullish AUD risk and fiscal factors, the still-positive absolute carry, and the live risk that a soft US PCE reverses the Fed-hike repricing and snaps AUD/USD higher. This is a measured Bearish lean, not a conviction short.
tactical.g10_relative_ranking: As an AUD-long expression, AUD/USD ranks among the WEAKEST of AUD's crosses tactically — behind AUD/JPY and AUD/CHF (where AUD's carry advantage is 6-8x larger and the funder is not a hawkish high-yielder) and behind short EUR/AUD (where the quote currency is a low-yielder, not a hawkish Fed). As a USD-short expression, AUD/USD ranks mid-pack: a hawkish-Fed dollar is better shorted via low-yielders only where carry is unfavourable, and against AUD the USD short loses the carry argument. Among the major USD pairs, the dollar's cleanest near-term strength expresses in USD/JPY (where carry, momentum and the +274bp 10Y gap align), making AUD/USD a secondary, lower-conviction dollar trade. Net: AUD/USD is neither the best AUD long nor the best USD short — a low-ranking, conflicted pair.

tactical.path_expectation: Expect a grind lower rather than a spike over 2-4 weeks, with rallies toward the 0.7120 descending-trendline resistance sold while the hawkish-Fed narrative is intact. The pair should probe the 0.6960-0.6945 200-day SMA region into the dense late-July data cluster, with the 25 June US core PCE and 2 July NFP the first tests and the 29 July AU CPI / FOMC double-header the decisive node. Volatility should stay contained (1M IV ~7.5%) unless a US inflation print surprises sharply or the ceasefire cracks. The pattern is a low-conviction corrective drift within the multi-month range, vulnerable to a sharp reversal higher on a single soft US data point — so the Bearish lean is best held with a defined invalidation above 0.7140. The carry cost of being short AUD/USD (~+4bp/month negative carry) is an ongoing drag that increases the urgency of the catalyst timeline; the late-July double-header is the key resolution point.

strategic.score_interpretation: Net score +0.076 sits squarely in the Neutral band, the product of an unusual symmetry: the two heavyweight factors (Policy & Rates 0.24, External Balance 0.16) both neutralise because both currencies are at peak rates and both carry comparable structural external drags. The residual tilt is faintly positive — USD Liquidity (+0.062, post-QT mid-smile) and Fiscal (+0.042, AAA vs Aa1) outweigh the Bearish Event Risk (-0.028, tariff cliff). This maps to conviction 2 — a genuinely balanced Neutral where the pair is anchored in its range by offsetting structural forces.
strategic.rationale: At strategic weights the dominant feature is symmetry: the 0.24 Policy & Rates factor neutralises because both central banks are at/past peak (RBA losing its oil-inflation hawkishness, Fed's edge fading to synchronized global tightening), and the heaviest non-rate factor, External Balance (0.16), also neutralises because Australia's -3.68% current account and rich REER (115.86) are offset by the US twin deficit, rich REER (107) and reserve diversification. The base and quote views interact to a wash: this is the rare pair where both currencies are simultaneously rich, high-rate, and structurally challenged, so neither wins decisively over 1-6 months. The residual lean is faintly AUD/USD-positive via USD Liquidity (post-QT mid-smile lets AUD carry express) and the AAA-vs-Aa1 Fiscal gap, partly offset by the doubly-Bearish 10 November tariff cliff. What is priced versus what we expect: consensus has AUD/USD range-bound at 0.69-0.73 (median ~0.71), essentially our base case, and the consensus projection places AUD/USD around 0.72 over the following 12 months, reflecting modest Aussie appreciation underpinned by continued RBA hawkishness. Our small edge is that the hawkish-Fed turn the consensus largely predates keeps the pair firmer-for-USD (lower) than that 0.72 path into Q3, before structural symmetry reasserts the range. The path of least resistance is a wide, range-bound drift centred on 0.69-0.71, with downside risk clustering around the 10 November tariff cliff. Key strategic catalysts: the 29 July, 16 September (SEP) and 28 October FOMCs, the 11 August and 3 November RBA SoMPs, the mid-August Iran deadline, and the 10 November tariff expiry. Conviction drivers: the structural symmetry that anchors the range; conviction cap (at 2): the genuine two-sided tail risk from the tariff cliff (Bearish) versus a dovish-Fed/politicised-cut scenario (Bullish). This is a low-conviction Neutral best traded as a range, not a trend.
strategic.g10_relative_ranking: Strategically AUD/USD is a low-ranking expression for both a long-AUD and a short-USD thesis. For AUD bulls, AUD/JPY and short EUR/AUD rank well above AUD/USD because they avoid the hawkish-Fed quote leg and harvest AUD's far superior carry against low-yielders. For USD bears, EUR/USD and NOK/USD rank above AUD/USD because the dollar-soft structural thesis (twin deficits, reserve diversification) expresses more cleanly against the surplus EUR and high-yield NOK than against an AUD that is itself rich and losing its rate edge. Among AUD crosses, AUD/USD ranks toward the bottom precisely because the quote currency is a hawkish high-yielder — the worst counterpart for an AUD carry trade. Net: a mid-to-low-ranking, range-bound pair with no clean strategic edge.

decrease_conditions: Decrease to 3 (Bearish) if the Fed delivers a clear hike while the RBA explicitly flags end-of-cycle, collapsing the differential below +30bp
Decrease to 1 (Bearish) if tariff re-escalation post-10 November triggers a China-growth scare AND a flight to USD drives AUD/USD below 0.6600

risk_reward.base_case_payoff: +100-200 pips toward 0.6850-0.6900 on the modest USD-firm-into-Q3 lean (less negative carry cost)
risk_reward.downside_case_loss: -285 pips on a re-widening differential toward 0.7300; -415 pips in the tail upside case
risk_reward.risk_reward_ratio: ~0.5:1 outright — poor, confirming range-trade not directional position
risk_reward.expected_value: (0.50 × +60) + (0.25 × +415 downside-case to 0.66) − (0.25 × +285 upside squeeze) ≈ +30 + 104 − 71 ≈ +63 pips, marginally positive but with large two-sided variance
risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Genuinely two-sided — the tariff/dovish-pivot tails roughly balance the Fed-hike/differential-collapse tails — which is why the strategic stance is a low-conviction Neutral best expressed via the range (sell 0.72, buy 0.69) or via relative-value crosses rather than an outright AUD/USD position.

consensus_divergence.bank_consensus_forecast: Major banks forecast AUD/USD in the 0.69 to 0.73 range; Westpac, NAB, and CBA project 0.69-0.72 with CBA allowing upside to 0.73; 12-month median consensus ~0.72 reflecting modest Aussie appreciation underpinned by continued RBA hawkishness (sources: Mitrade, Discovery Alert, FXStreet)
consensus_divergence.our_tactical_view: Mildly Bearish AUD/USD; drift toward 0.6950-0.7000 over 2-4 weeks; conviction 2
consensus_divergence.our_strategic_view: Neutral; wide range 0.6900-0.7100, centred on ~0.70; conviction 2
consensus_divergence.divergence_direction: Below consensus on the tactical horizon; in-line with the lower end of consensus strategically
consensus_divergence.divergence_magnitude: ~200 pips below consensus 0.72 at the tactical horizon; ~100 pips below the 0.71 range midpoint strategically
consensus_divergence.why_we_differ: We weight the hawkish-Fed regime change more heavily than the bank forecasts, most of which were set earlier in 2026 and embed two assumptions now stale: continued RBA hawkishness (eroding as oil collapses) and a dovish/cutting Fed (now flipped to a likely hike at 77% probability). The bilateral carry edge is being compressed from BOTH sides, not just supported by RBA hawkishness. As one desk put it, AUD/USD is probably the best high-beta FX expression of a hawkish Fed — a characterisation that banks with higher forecasts appear to underweight. We agree on the range but are more cautious about the near-term upside path given the carry compression and negative AUD positioning momentum.
consensus_divergence.payoff_if_right: A tactical short AUD/USD or long AUD/JPY (avoiding the hawkish-Fed quote leg) outperforms a consensus positioned for 0.72 — potentially ~200 pips of edge on the tactical horizon
consensus_divergence.risk_if_wrong: If post-ceasefire disinflation hits the US fast and the Fed reverts to cuts, the consensus 0.72 path materialises and the pair recovers toward 0.71-0.72, stopping out the Bearish lean with a ~100-200 pip loss

cross_horizon_consistency.explanation: Tactical Bearish (conviction 2) shades to strategic Neutral (conviction 2) — broadly aligned in low conviction, with the tactical lean being the near-term hawkish-Fed compression of AUD's carry edge, which neutralises strategically as both central banks reach peak and the structural symmetry (both currencies rich, high-rate, externally challenged) reasserts the range. The transition from tactical Bearish to strategic Neutral is driven by the normalization of the hawkish-Fed surprise over 1-6 months as post-ceasefire disinflation data accumulates.
cross_horizon_consistency.inflection_point: 29 July AU Q2 CPI / FOMC double-header is the nearest tactical node; 16 September Fed SEP is the primary strategic inflection when the durability of the hike and the pace of post-ceasefire disinflation will be clarified
cross_horizon_consistency.trading_implication: Run the tactical AUD/USD-Bearish lean with a tight leash and a defined exit above 0.7140, then rotate to a range-trading / relative-value (long AUD/JPY, short EUR/AUD) stance for the strategic horizon, adding cheap AUD/USD downside hedges ahead of the 10 November tariff cliff

forward_vs_spot.forward_1m: Outright ~0.7011; forward points ~-4.0 pips; implied move -0.72%/yr (AUD at forward discount, CIP-consistent given AU-US short rates)
forward_vs_spot.edge_3m: ~-12 pips (we expect spot mildly below the forward — slightly more Bearish than the carry-implied path)
forward_vs_spot.edge_6m: ~+61 pips (we expect spot above the forward by year-end as the strategic range reasserts and the Fed-hike narrative fades)

catalysts.event: US Core PCE (May) & Q1 GDP third estimate
catalysts.rationale: Core PCE is the Fed's preferred inflation gauge; a sticky print (~3.3%) validates the hawkish dot plot and the front-end repricing, lifting the USD and pressuring AUD/USD. A soft print would be the first crack in the hike thesis and lift AUD/USD sharply. This is the first test of the hawkish-Fed narrative that drives the pair's near-term direction. The bilateral transmission is direct: a hot print compresses the AU-US rate differential further from the quote side, reducing AUD's carry edge and reinforcing the Bearish lean.

Australian unemployment stood at 4.49%; a tight print supports the eroding RBA hawkish path and AUD (Bullish AUD/USD), while a soft print revives easing speculation and pressures the base leg (Bearish AUD/USD). This is a smaller swing event than the US data on the same day, but it matters bilaterally because the RBA's data-dependence means a labour-market surprise could either extend or accelerate the carry-fade narrative that is the key tactical driver for the pair.

NFP is the highest-impact US release; a firm print (after +172k) keeps the Fed hawkish and the USD bid, pressuring AUD/USD via the quote leg. A soft print reopens the cut narrative, unwinds USD longs and lifts the pair sharply. The bilateral importance is acute: payrolls directly determine whether the 29 July FOMC confirms the hike path, making this an indirect but powerful AUD/USD catalyst through the quote-side rate channel.

Tests whether cheaper post-ceasefire oil is cooling US inflation sufficiently to undercut the Fed-hike rationale. A still-hot core CPI keeps the Fed hawkish and the USD firm, capping AUD/USD via the quote leg. A clear deceleration would undercut the hike narrative and lift the pair materially. The bilateral transmission: this print directly determines the 29 July FOMC outcome, which is the same day as the AU Q2 CPI — making this the second leg of the late-July AUD/USD catalyst cluster.

catalysts.event: ABS Q2 CPI (Australia, headline + trimmed mean)
catalysts.rationale: The single highest-impact AUD event. With oil collapsing, fuel-driven inflation is fading and the print is skewed to a soft, disinflationary surprise that would confirm the RBA carry fade and pressure AUD directly (Bearish AUD/USD). A soft AU CPI on the same day as the FOMC creates an amplifying double-header: the base leg of the pair is hit by confirmed RBA dovishness at the exact moment the quote leg is repriced by the Fed — a uniquely concentrated bilateral stress point for AUD/USD.

First meeting where the June dot-plot hike could materialise. Policymakers are expected to leave the target range unchanged while keeping the option of further tightening on the table if inflation remains stubborn. A hawkish hold sustains the USD (Bearish AUD/USD); an actual hike pushes the pair sharply lower by collapsing the AU-US differential from the quote side. Coincides with the AU CPI on the same day — a decisive double-header that represents the peak bilateral stress moment in the tactical window.

A hawkish BoJ step or intervention near USD/JPY 161 would squeeze the crowded short-JPY trade and trigger broad risk-off, hitting high-beta AUD/USD via the risk channel even though neither leg of AUD/USD is directly involved. The bilateral importance is indirect but real: AUD is the most liquid high-beta proxy in Asia-Pacific G10 and a BoJ-driven risk-off sells AUD as part of the high-beta unwind while the USD benefits from the flight-to-quality channel, producing a net AUD/USD-negative impulse.

catalysts.event: RBA Decision + Statement on Monetary Policy (August SoMP)
catalysts.rationale: First SoMP since the oil collapse; a dovish forecast revision acknowledging cheaper oil and faster disinflation would confirm the RBA carry fade and pressure AUD directly (Bearish AUD/USD). A reaffirmed hike bias would be Bullish AUD/USD. This is the key base-leg event that will clarify whether the RBA's hawkish hold on 16 June is the last of the cycle or a temporary pause, with direct implications for the AU-US rate differential and the pair's carry foundation.

A durable deal cements risk-on (Bullish AUD/USD via high-beta AUD appreciation and drained USD safe-haven bid); a breakdown restores the USD haven bid and re-spikes oil (Bearish AUD/USD via both the haven channel and the reimposition of inflation risk). The bilateral transmission is doubly powerful for AUD/USD: the risk channel hits AUD's high-beta nature while the haven channel lifts USD simultaneously, making a breakdown asymmetrically more negative for AUD/USD than for low-beta USD pairs. The market currently prices a durable outcome, leaving the breakdown tail underpriced.

catalysts.event: FOMC Meeting + Summary of Economic Projections (SEP)
catalysts.rationale: Fresh dot plot; whether the hike survives depends on whether oil-driven disinflation has appeared in the data by mid-September. This is the meeting most likely to flip the pair from USD-firm to range-bound, and represents the primary strategic inflection point. If the Fed walks back the hike on visible disinflation, AUD/USD recovers toward 0.71-0.72; if the Fed hikes, the pair probes 0.68. The bilateral importance is maximum because this SEP will define the US-AU rate differential trajectory for the remainder of 2026.

A US government shutdown or messy continuing resolution injects Treasury-market and political volatility into the pre-midterm window — a mild USD-negative that would lift AUD/USD via the quote leg. Australia has no equivalent fiscal cliff, creating a clear bilateral asymmetry that temporarily favours the base currency. This is a low-probability high-impact event that is currently not priced, providing a modest upside catalyst for AUD/USD in the strategic horizon.

The single largest dated AUD/USD-specific risk. Re-escalation hits Chinese growth and Australian bulk-commodity demand directly (Bearish AUD via the China-proxy channel) AND simultaneously restores the USD safe-haven bid (Bearish from the quote side) — doubly negative for AUD/USD in a way that does not apply to most other G10 pairs. Australia is the most liquid China proxy in G10 FX; tariff re-escalation that reduces Chinese iron ore and coal demand is a direct terms-of-trade shock to the base currency. An extension of the truce removes the key strategic left-tail for the pair.

Year-end meeting and final 2026 dot plot; sets the 2027 path for the US rate cycle. A pivot toward the consensus 2027-cut narrative would lift AUD/USD by re-widening the AU-US differential from the quote side; a sustained hawkish path would cap the pair at the lower half of the 0.69-0.72 range. The bilateral importance is structural: this meeting determines whether the AUD/USD carry trade thesis is rehabilitated for 2027 or whether the Fed's hawkish posture persists into the new year, directly affecting the pair's strategic direction.

invalidators.condition: Fed delivers a 25bp hike at the July or September FOMC while the RBA holds at 4.35%, collapsing the AU-US 2Y spread below +30bp
invalidators.mechanism: The AU-US front-end differential collapses toward parity, removing AUD's last bilateral rate support against the dollar; the carry breakeven tightens from ~42 pips to near-zero, eliminating any carry cushion on AUD/USD dips and turning the rate factor from a mild headwind to a decisive Bearish driver
invalidators.monitor_indicator: FOMC decision and AU-US 2Y yield spread
invalidators.monitor_frequency: Per meeting; daily for the yield spread

invalidators.condition: US core PCE prints below 2.8% YoY on cheaper oil, reversing the Fed-hike repricing
invalidators.mechanism: The Fed-hike rationale evaporates, front-end US rates reprice lower, USD longs unwind, and the AU-US 2Y differential re-widens in AUD's favour — directly reversing the dominant bearish driver. AUD/USD would likely snap above 0.7140, flipping CTAs long and triggering systematic inflow

invalidators.condition: US-China tariff truce lapses after 10 November AND iron ore falls below $85/t
invalidators.mechanism: The pair is hit on both legs simultaneously — Australia's China-export proxy is impaired (Bearish AUD) while the USD safe-haven bid is restored (Bullish USD) — a uniquely adverse double-hit for AUD/USD that does not apply to most other G10 pairs. The iron ore threshold confirms the real-economy transmission to Australian terms of trade
invalidators.monitor_indicator: Tariff headlines (US-China), iron ore 62% Fe CFR China, CSI 300 index

invalidators.condition: A politicised Fed rate cut occurs under perceived political pressure from the administration
invalidators.mechanism: The dollar's institutional-credibility premium erodes via the quote leg — a rapid USD depreciation unrelated to AUD fundamentals lifts AUD/USD regardless of the rate differential. This is an asymmetric, underpriced tail that operates through the credibility and safe-haven channels simultaneously, and would likely be accompanied by broader USD weakness across G10
invalidators.monitor_indicator: Fed communications, administration statements on monetary policy, USD DXY

invalidators.condition: AUD/USD breaks and holds below the 0.6945 200-day SMA on a weekly close
invalidators.mechanism: The structural uptrend breaks and CTAs flip short, accelerating the move via systematic flow — a pair-specific technical trigger that operates independently of either currency's fundamentals. The 200d is the key CTA trigger for this pair; a weekly close break confirms the trend change and typically brings 2-3% of follow-through before the next structural support
invalidators.monitor_indicator: AUD/USD weekly close vs 200-day moving average (0.6966)

invalidators.condition: AU-US 2Y spread re-widens above +100bp as the Fed cuts faster than the RBA
invalidators.mechanism: The carry edge that anchors the AUD bull case is restored from the quote side — reversing the dominant bearish driver that has been compressing the differential from below. A +100bp AU-US 2Y spread would restore the carry-to-vol ratio to above 1.0 (attractive), reignite the AUD/USD carry trade, and likely coincide with a broad USD unwind that amplifies the directional move

signposts.confirms_if: Compresses below +30bp — confirming the bilateral carry collapse thesis and validating the Bearish tactical stance
signposts.invalidates_if: Re-widens above +75bp — indicating the AU-US differential is expanding in AUD's favour, invalidating the rate-compression thesis
signposts.bilateral_importance: This is THE core AUD/USD carry driver and the pair's most direct rate signal — it matters more for AUD/USD than for any AUD cross because the quote currency itself carries a top-tier rate, so the spread is squeezed from both sides (RBA losing its hawkish premise AND Fed hiking). No other AUD cross faces this bilateral compression dynamic.

signposts.confirms_if: Rises above 85% — confirming the Fed-hike compression of AUD carry and reinforcing the Bearish tactical stance
signposts.invalidates_if: Falls below 40% — indicating a repricing toward holds/cuts that would allow AUD/USD to recover toward 0.7140 and above
signposts.bilateral_importance: The hawkish-Fed quote leg is the dominant near-term AUD/USD driver this cycle — more so than for low-beta USD pairs because AUD/USD is the cleanest high-beta expression of a hawkish Fed. A shift in hike probability is immediately transmitted to AUD/USD positioning through the high-beta risk channel and the carry channel simultaneously.

signposts.confirms_if: Falls below $85/t — signalling a China demand slowdown that would directly impair AUD's terms of trade and base-leg carry foundation
signposts.invalidates_if: Rises above $110/t — indicating a China stimulus reflation that would lift AUD's commodity support and revive the bull case for the base leg
signposts.bilateral_importance: Iron ore is Australia's dominant export (~35% of revenue) and oil-insensitive; for AUD/USD specifically it matters more than for AUD crosses because the USD is a net energy exporter, so iron ore is the cleaner AUD-specific terms-of-trade signal independent of the energy swing. A soft iron ore print is AUD-negative without any USD offset, making it the purest base-leg fundamental signal for this pair.

signposts.confirms_if: Sustained break below 0.6945 on a weekly close — confirming the structural uptrend break and triggering CTA short-selling flow that would accelerate the move
signposts.invalidates_if: Holds above 0.7120 descending-trendline resistance on a closing basis — signalling the corrective phase is over and rallies have resumed
signposts.bilateral_importance: The 200d SMA is the structural trend/CTA trigger for this specific pair; a break flips systematic trend-followers short and accelerates the move via systematic flow — a pair-specific flow dynamic that amplifies any fundamental bearish signal. This trigger is absent from the standalone dossiers and only emerges at the bilateral pair level.

signposts.confirms_if: Steepens beyond -1.00 (rising downside-hedge demand confirms the Bearish skew is intensifying)
signposts.invalidates_if: Flattens toward -0.30 (downside hedging easing, indicating risk-on and/or dovish-Fed repricing are supporting AUD/USD)
signposts.bilateral_importance: The skew captures BOTH AUD's high-beta downside and the hawkish-Fed USD-strength tail simultaneously — a uniquely informative options signal for this specific pair that does not exist in the same form for AUD crosses. The risk reversal is both a sentiment gauge and a real-money hedging signal; steepening beyond -1.00 would indicate professional hedgers are loading up on AUD/USD downside protection, validating the Bearish tactical lean.

signposts.confirms_if: Spikes above 22 (risk-off bids USD haven while selling high-beta AUD — a doubly negative bilateral effect for AUD/USD)
signposts.invalidates_if: Stays below 16 (sustained risk-on supports AUD's high-beta premium and drains the USD safe-haven bid, supporting AUD/USD)
signposts.bilateral_importance: AUD/USD is a moderate risk-beta pair where a VIX spike is doubly negative — it simultaneously sells high-beta AUD (base leg) AND bids the USD safe haven (quote leg) — so VIX matters more for the directional signal here than for low-beta crosses like EUR/USD. The VIX threshold of 22 is the historical level at which AUD/USD systematic selling tends to accelerate based on the 2022-2025 risk-off episodes.

assumptions: Spot (0.7015), performance, 52-week range and moving averages (50d 0.7138, 100d 0.7089, 200d 0.6966) are taken from the supplied Twelve Data OHLC as the source of truth; minor discrepancy with dossier narrative MAs (~0.708/0.695/0.670) which appear stale — price data used throughout per framework instructions.

Both dossiers cite slightly different spot conventions (AUD dossier 0.70695; USD dossier 0.7069); the Twelve Data latest close 0.7015 (2026-06-19) is used as authoritative for all price-derived metrics.
Bilateral carry, breakeven and forward figures are derived from the rate differentials in both dossiers; forward points are CIP-consistent approximations, not live terminal quotes.

Support/resistance levels and chart patterns are sourced from web technical analysis (MarketPulse/OANDA, FXStreet, ActionForex) as these require visual chart reading; trend regime and MAs are from price data.

Risk reversal (-0.71), IV (1M 7.5%, 3M 7.81%) and IV percentile (~35th) are carried from the AUD dossier's Saxo-sourced snapshot as no fresher live AUD/USD options print was available in search; flagged as indicative.
Bilateral trade data is taken directly from the supplied IMF DOTS block (AUD net goods balance vs USD -$19.9bn, 2024) and not re-estimated.

Default factor weights applied throughout; the key interpretive choice is treating this as a 'both-dossiers-Bullish, signal-muted' pair where the heavier-weight, larger-surprise factor (the hawkish Fed) breaks the tie tactically, while structural symmetry produces a strategic Neutral.
The Iran ceasefire duration is assumed to be the base case (60-day truce holds through mid-August); a breakdown is treated as a tail scenario in the risk assessment.

The 10 November US-China tariff truce expiry date is treated as the dominant strategic event risk; the assumption is that the market currently underprices this tail, reflecting the base-case assumption that the truce will be extended.

CFTC positioning data is from the week to 9 June 2026 (most recent available); there is a ~1-week lag that may mean some further AUD long liquidation post-FOMC is not yet reflected in the cited figures.
